summary:
The most likely cause for a child presenting with severe nighttime pruritus localized around the interdigital areas accompanied by visible burrow marks would be scabies based upon classic presentation patterns observed clinically across multiple studies. Scabies' hallmark features align perfectly here – nocturnal itchiness peaking during sleep alongside distinctive skin lesions indicative thereof. Thus, this condition stands out as highly plausible given all provided information about the case scenario described above involving our young patient experiencing distressing dermatological issues suggestive specifically of mite infestation via tunnel formation beneath epidermis layers typical only within context defined herein regarding suspected parasitic infection known medically under name 'scabies'. To conclude succinctly:

The primary suspicion should rest firmly on diagnosing *Scabies* due its precise match against established diagnostic criteria including specific distribution pattern noted along with telltale signs such as intensely itchy episodes especially after dark coupled together forming clear picture fitting well into recognized medical literature guidelines pertaining said disease entity characterized fundamentally through presence microscopic ectoparasites Trichinella Serrata causing cutaneous eruptions manifest visually observable worm tracks termed ‘burrows’.
